title: On random walks in the group of Euclidean isometries
speaker: Elon Lindenstrauss
abstract: In contrast to the two dimensional case, in dimension $d \geq 3$ averaging operators on the $d-1$-sphere using finitely many rotations, i.e. averaging operators of the form $Af(x)= |S|^{-1} \sum_{\theta \in S} f(s x)$ where $S$ is a finite subset of $\mathrm{SO}(d)$, can have a spectral gap on $L^2$ of the $d - 1$-sphere. A result of Bourgain and Gamburd shows that this holds, for instance, for any finite set of elements in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ with algebraic entries and spanning a dense subgroup.

proof_stages.label: Euclidean-isometry random-walk setup
proof_stages.summary: The walk is defined by i.i.d. orientation-preserving Euclidean isometries, with rotation and translation parts separated, dense generated subgroup, no common fixed point, and an expansion of the endpoint as a correlated sum of bounded terms.

proof_stages.label: Rotational spectral-gap hypothesis
proof_stages.summary: The key assumption is a spectral gap for the averaging operator generated by the rotation parts on L^2(SO(d)) off the constants, a condition meaningful in dimensions d at least three and known from explicit and algebraic constructions.

proof_stages.label: Fourier-side spherical representations
proof_stages.summary: Fourier transform converts the Euclidean-isometry action on L^2(R^d) into a direct integral of representations H_r on spheres, where rotations act on the sphere and translations appear as phases.

proof_stages.label: Weak two-radius spectral-gap input
proof_stages.summary: A preliminary estimate says that for two nearby radii at least one associated H_r-operator has an effective norm drop, because spectral-gap uniqueness of almost-invariant vectors is incompatible with changing phases.

proof_stages.label: Fourier nonconcentration from the weak gap
proof_stages.summary: The needed small-ball nonconcentration is reduced to bounds on the Fourier transform of ν_n convolved with its reflection, using shell averages, the weak gap’s good radii, and decomposition into near-origin and far-origin parts.

proof_stages.label: Bourgain–Gamburd bootstrap to uniform spectral gap
proof_stages.summary: The core new theorem is a uniform spectral-gap estimate for the averaging operators on H_r away from r=0, obtained by feeding nonconcentration into a Bourgain–Gamburd bootstrapping argument adapted to the noncompact Euclidean-isometry setting.

proof_stages.label: Consequences for local limits and self-similar measures
proof_stages.summary: The uniform spectral gap, combined with earlier work of Varjú and auxiliary lemmas, yields an exponential-resolution quantitative local central limit theorem and small-ball bounds for the random walk, and also smoothness of certain self-similar measures with rotations.

examples.name: Translation-only random walk on Euclidean space
examples.note: The usual random walk obtained by taking only translations is mentioned as a familiar special case of the Euclidean-isometry random walk, not as a source of new input.
examples.engagement_reason: The translation-only case is identified as the usual Euclidean random walk, but the speaker explicitly says they will not say anything new about it and does not analyze it.

examples.note: Test functions concentrated on exponentially small balls illustrate how the theorem probes the distribution at exponentially fine spatial resolution.

examples.name: The two-dimensional solvable/abelian rotation case
examples.note: The contrast with d=2, where the rotation group is abelian and the isometry group is solvable, illustrates why the spectral-gap phenomenon belongs to dimensions at least three.
examples.engagement_reason: The two-dimensional case is discussed only as a solvable/abelian rotation-group contrast and as a historically known case, without carrying out an argument on it.

examples.name: Finite-valued independent random walk obstruction
examples.note: A finite-valued independent random walk has only polynomially many possible endpoints at time n, illustrating why exponential-resolution Gaussian approximation cannot come merely from independence.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker gives a concrete counting obstruction for finite-valued independent walks, noting at most polynomially many endpoints and explaining why probing below that scale jams.

passes.reason: A standard finite-support independent walk is used to show exponential-scale Gaussian probing would fail because only polynomially many endpoints occur.
The finite-valued independent walk is used as an obstruction showing that such strong small-scale probing would fail without the rotational correlations.

examples.note: Rotations about coordinate axes by a special angle give an explicit spectral-gap set, showing that the spectral-gap hypothesis is non-vacuous.

examples.name: Equal contraction ratios in the self-similar measure application
examples.note: The speaker notes that the Fourier-decay argument would be easier when all contraction ratios are the same, serving as a simplifying special case of the self-similar-measure application.
examples.engagement_reason: For equal contraction ratios the speaker traces the Fourier-shell relation, explains how spectral gap gives Fourier decay, and how that yields smoothness.

passes.reason: Using one common contraction ratio is the homogeneous regular case that makes the Fourier-shell argument straightforward.
Equal contraction ratios are the homogeneous regular case of the self-similar-measure argument, making the Fourier-shell reduction straightforward.
